draft.introduction: John 13:1–20 narrates the foot-washing, which replaces the eucharistic institution in the Fourth Gospel’s Last Supper account. Jesus performs a slave’s task to demonstrate the nature of divine love and servant leadership.

translation.text: Now before the feast of the Passover, when Jesus knew that his hour had come to depart out of this world to the Father, having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end. He rose from supper, laid aside his outer garments, and taking a towel, tied it around his waist. Then he poured water into a basin and began to wash the disciples’ feet.
translation.original_text: πρὸ δὲ τῆς ἑορτῆς τοῦ πάσχα εἰδὼς ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι ἦλθεν αὐτοῦ ἡ ὥρα

translator_notes.note: The phrase “loved them to the end” (εἰς τέλος ἀγάπησεν) carries a double meaning in Greek: both “to the end of his life” and “to the uttermost.” John’s replacement of the eucharist with foot-washing reframes sacrifice as humble service.
